passage:
A wildlife biologist tracked the average territory size (in square kilometers) of a predatory bird species and compared it to prey density (small mammals per km²) across five habitats.

| Habitat | Prey Density (mammals/km²) | Avg. Territory Size (km²) |
|---|---|---|
| Wetland | 42 | 8 |
| Grassland | 31 | 14 |
| Scrubland | 24 | 21 |
| Forest Edge | 18 | 29 |
| Open Desert | 11 | 38 |

The biologist hypothesized that predatory birds establish larger territories in areas with lower prey density, as they must range more widely to find adequate food.
question: Which statement most directly supports the biologist's hypothesis?

choices.A: The Open Desert habitat has the largest average territory at 38 km².
choices.B: The Wetland habitat has the highest prey density at 42 mammals/km².
choices.C: As prey density decreases from 42 (Wetland) to 11 (Open Desert), average territory size increases from 8 to 38 km²—a consistent inverse relationship across all five habitats.
choices.D: Forest Edge and Open Desert habitats have larger territories than Grassland.

explanation: The biologist's hypothesis posits an inverse relationship: lower prey density corresponds to larger territory size. The best evidence for this relationship shows it holding consistently across the data, not just at one point. Choice C identifies the full pattern across all five habitats: as prey density falls from 42 to 11, territory size rises from 8 to 38—a consistent inverse relationship with no exceptions. This directly supports the hypothesis. Choice A notes only the extreme for one variable. Choice B notes only the extreme for the other variable. Choice D makes a partial comparison (Forest Edge and Open Desert vs. Grassland only) without establishing the full inverse pattern or connecting both variables.
